excerpt: Prayer is not without purity (lā ṣalāh illā bi-l-ṭuhūr)
Whoever completes his purification and then goes out from his house to the mosque to pray, that believer remains in a state of prayer until his ablution is broken

The perfection of ablution is achieved by repeating each member three times. Water represents the graces divided into three parts. Knowledge is similarly divided into three parts: jurisprudence, esoteric exegesis, and inner truths. Washing each organ three times signifies recognition of the three ranks: the Speaker, the Foundation, and the Imam
source_work: Raḥat al-ʿAql (The Repose of the Intellect)

excerpt: This is my ablution and the ablution of the Prophets whose leader I am
The seven organs of ablution correspond to seven stations in the hierarchy of faith, seven Imams, and seven foundations
source_location_hint: section on the hierarchy of ḥudūd

excerpt: The three Sunna organs are exemplified by the three ranks of the Summoner, the Proof, and the Imam, while the four obligatory organs exemplify the four principles: the cosmic intellect, the cosmic soul, the Speaker, and the Foundation
source_work: al-Masābīḥ fī Ithbāt al-Imāma (Lamps on the Proof of the Imamate)

excerpt: Pure water is a symbol of knowledge. Just as the impurity of the body is washed away by water, so the impurity of the self is washed away by knowledge. The taking of a covenant is the essence of purity, and knowledge of truth is the invitation to prayer
source_work: Taʾwīl al-Daʿāʾim (Esoteric Exegesis of the Ordinances)
source_location_hint: section on esoteric meaning of wuḍūʾ

excerpt: The bucket by which the seeker draws water from the well is an analogy for the knowledge of the Summoner, who reads the knowledge of the Proof and teaches it to others. The vessel is an analogy for the Universal Summoner
source_location_hint: section on vessels and vessels of knowledge

excerpt: When the verse was revealed, the Messenger of God took ʿAlī, Fāṭima, Ḥasan, and Ḥusayn under his cloak and declared: 'These are my People of the House'
source_work: Ḥadīth al-Kisāʾ (The Tradition of the Cloak)

excerpt: In the verse of purification, the word 'impurity' refers to false doctrines and polytheism of believers regarding the knowledge of the Imams. God has resolved to correct these false beliefs through the knowledge of esoteric exegesis and inner truths

Heaven refers to the Prophet Muhammad. Earth refers to his Successor ʿAlī. Water refers to knowledge. The earth absorbs water and produces vegetation—a symbol of the transmission and cultivation of knowledge through the Imam and his Proof
source_work: Jamiʿ al-Ḥikmatayn (The Harmony of Two Wisdoms)
source_location_hint: section on celestial and terrestrial knowledge

excerpt: Bad earth is an analogy for the corrupt among the community. When pure knowledge reached them, they altered and corrupted it. The zodiac is an analogy for salty water, and its followers are creatures dependent only upon this salt water
source_location_hint: majlis on knowledge and its corruption

excerpt: Clouds that rain are like Summoners of the Pen—those who write books and disseminate knowledge. Those that only thunder are like Summoners of the Sword—those who struggle with the sword. Both purpose to purify souls
source_location_hint: section on metaphors of knowledge transmission

excerpt: The sun draws water from the earth as vapor and transforms it into pure water through clouds. The sun is the Imam; the earth is ʿAlī's knowledge; the vapors are the eloquent words of the Nahj al-Balāgha; the water is accessible knowledge distributed to believers

In the early days of Islam, the Prophet used to wash his head and feet. After the Night Journey, when the verse on ablution was revealed, he changed his practice and began to wipe his head and feet instead

Wiping signifies acknowledgment of being; washing signifies knowledge of station. The parts washed (face, hands) are material limits (Speaker, Foundation); the parts wiped (head, feet) are spiritual limits (cosmic intellect, cosmic soul). Some limits require complete understanding; for others, partial acknowledgment suffices
source_location_hint: section on masḥ and ghasl distinction

excerpt: Concealment is permissible in all matters except three: the consumption of intoxicants, wiping over socks, and abandoning the Bismillāh in prayer. Imam Jaʿfar al-Ṣādiq ruled that wiping over socks renders prayer invalid
source_location_hint: canonical ruling on taqiyyah and wuḍūʾ

excerpt: The sick are those whose religious concepts are corrupted; the traveler is one far from the beneficial Imam; the one from the toilet is one whose thoughts are filled with doubt. Esoteric purification should occur independently, away from others' knowledge
source_location_hint: section on tayammum and spiritual purification

excerpt: Tayammum is performed on face and both hands, representing two stations of faith: distancing from ignorance and entering faith through covenant. One should not perform tayammum in the presence of a Benefactor who possesses pure water
